Assignment 1¶

I started by importing the libraries I needed for this project and loading the dataset.

In [43]:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split
from sklearn.tree import DecisionTreeClassifier, plot_tree
from sklearn.metrics import (accuracy_score, precision_score, recall_score,
                             f1_score, classification_report,
                             ConfusionMatrixDisplay)

I downloaded the dataset as a zip file from Kaggle. The code below uploads it in Google Colab, extracts the CSV, and reads it into a dataframe.

In [44]:
from google.colab import files
import zipfile, io

uploaded = files.upload()
filename = list(uploaded.keys())[0]

if filename.endswith('.zip'):
    with zipfile.ZipFile(io.BytesIO(uploaded[filename]), 'r') as z:
        csv_files = [f for f in z.namelist() if f.endswith('.csv')]
        with z.open(csv_files[0]) as csv_file:
            df = pd.read_csv(csv_file, encoding='latin-1')
else:
    df = pd.read_csv(io.BytesIO(uploaded[filename]), encoding='latin-1')

print(f"Dataset loaded: {df.shape[0]} rows, {df.shape[1]} columns")
df.head()

5 rows × 31 columns

Business Understanding¶

I worked on a dataset from the used car auction industry. Dealers buy cars at auction and sometimes end up with vehicles that have hidden problems like odometer tampering, engine damage, or title issues. These cars are called "kicks" or "bad buys" and they cost dealers money since they already paid for the car, transported it, and sometimes tried to fix it before realizing it was a lemon.

I framed this as a binary classification problem. The Class column is 0 for a good buy and 1 for a kick. The goal was to build a model that predicts whether a car will turn out to be a kick using information available at auction time like age, mileage, price, and make. If it works, dealers could use it as a screening tool to avoid risky purchases or at least bid lower on flagged cars.

Data Understanding¶

Variable Definitions¶

The dataset comes from a Carvana/Kaggle competition called "Don't Get Kicked." Here is what each variable means:

PurchDate: when the car was bought at auction Auction: which auction company sold it (Manheim, ADESA, etc.) VehYear: the year the car was manufactured VehicleAge: how old the car is in years Make: the brand (Ford, Chevy, Dodge, etc.) Model: specific model name Trim: trim level (SE, LX, Limited, etc.) SubModel: more specific version info Color: exterior color Transmission: automatic or manual WheelTypeID: numeric code for wheel type WheelType: wheel description (alloy, covers, etc.) VehOdo: odometer reading in miles Nationality: where the manufacturer is from (American, Asian, etc.) Size: vehicle size category (compact, medium, SUV, truck, etc.) TopThreeAmericanName: whether it is one of the big 3 US brands (GM, Ford, Chrysler) MMRAcquisitionAuctionAveragePrice: market value at auction, average condition MMRAcquisitionAuctionCleanPrice: market value at auction, above-average condition MMRAcquisitionRetailAveragePrice: retail market value, average condition MMRAcquisitonRetailCleanPrice: retail market value, above-average condition MMRCurrentAuctionAveragePrice: current auction value, average condition MMRCurrentAuctionCleanPrice: current auction value, above-average condition MMRCurrentRetailAveragePrice: current retail value, average condition MMRCurrentRetailCleanPrice: current retail value, above-average condition BYRNO: buyer ID number VNZIP1: ZIP code of the purchase location VNST: state where it was purchased VehBCost: what the dealer paid for the car IsOnlineSale: whether it was bought online WarrantyCost: cost of the warranty (36 months / 36K miles) Class: the target variable, 1 means kick (bad buy) and 0 means good buy

Relationship to the Problem¶

A lot of these variables seemed relevant to predicting kicks. Older cars with more miles are generally riskier. The price variables could reveal if a car was undervalued, which might signal problems. Make and size might matter if certain types of cars are more prone to hidden issues. The auction provider and state could capture regional patterns.

Summary Statistics¶

The dataset had a good number of rows and columns. There were no missing values in any of the columns. I moved on to visualizing the data.

Chart 1 - Class Distribution¶

In [48]:
counts = df['Class'].value_counts().sort_index()
labels = ['Good Buy (0)', 'Kick (1)']

plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
plt.bar(labels, counts.values, color=['steelblue', 'coral'], edgecolor='black')
plt.title('Count of Good Buys vs Kicks')
plt.ylabel('Number of Vehicles')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print(f"Kick rate: {counts[1] / counts.sum() * 100:.2f}%")

Kick rate: 9.55%

The data was heavily imbalanced. The vast majority of cars were good buys and only a small fraction were kicks. This makes sense for the real world but it meant a model could just predict good buy every time and still get high accuracy. I kept this in mind when evaluating the models later.

Chart 2 - Vehicle Age Distribution¶

In [49]:
plt.figure(figsize=(7, 4))
plt.hist(df['VehicleAge'].dropna(), bins=15, color='steelblue', edgecolor='black')
plt.title('Distribution of Vehicle Age')
plt.xlabel('Vehicle Age (years)')
plt.ylabel('Frequency')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Most cars at auction were around 3 to 5 years old, which makes sense since that is when leases end and rental fleets turn over.

Chart 3 - Kick Rate by Vehicle Age¶

In [50]:
age_kick = df.groupby('VehicleAge')['Class'].mean() * 100

plt.figure(figsize=(7, 4))
plt.bar(age_kick.index, age_kick.values, color='coral', edgecolor='black')
plt.title('Kick Rate by Vehicle Age')
plt.xlabel('Vehicle Age (years)')
plt.ylabel('Kick Rate (%)')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

The kick rate generally increased with vehicle age. A 3-year-old car had a lower chance of being a kick compared to a 7 or 8-year-old one. This confirmed that age is a useful predictor.

Chart 4 - Odometer Distribution¶

In [51]:
plt.figure(figsize=(7, 4))
plt.hist(df['VehOdo'].dropna(), bins=40, color='mediumpurple', edgecolor='black')
plt.title('Distribution of Odometer Reading')
plt.xlabel('Miles')
plt.ylabel('Frequency')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Odometer readings were clustered around 70,000 to 80,000 miles, which is typical for used auction cars. Higher mileage generally means more wear and tear and a higher chance of problems.

Chart 5 - Top 10 Makes by Count¶

In [52]:
top10 = df['Make'].value_counts().head(10)

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
top10.plot(kind='barh', color='teal', edgecolor='black')
plt.title('Top 10 Makes by Volume')
plt.xlabel('Count')
plt.gca().invert_yaxis()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Chevy, Dodge, and Ford dominated the auction market since they are the most common brands in the US. When I checked the kick rates by make, some brands had noticeably higher kick rates than others, so the make of the car seemed worth keeping as a feature.

Chart 6 - Kick Rate by Auction Provider¶

In [53]:
auction_kick = df.groupby('Auction')['Class'].mean() * 100

plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
auction_kick.plot(kind='bar', color='salmon', edgecolor='black')
plt.title('Kick Rate by Auction Provider')
plt.ylabel('Kick Rate (%)')
plt.xticks(rotation=0)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

There were some differences in kick rates across auction providers. This could reflect different quality standards or the types of vehicles each provider tends to handle. It seemed like a useful variable to include.

Chart 7 - Kick Rate by Vehicle Size¶

In [54]:
size_kick = df.groupby('Size')['Class'].mean() * 100
size_kick = size_kick.sort_values(ascending=False)

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 4))
plt.bar(size_kick.index, size_kick.values, color='orchid', edgecolor='black')
plt.title('Kick Rate by Vehicle Size')
plt.xlabel('Size')
plt.ylabel('Kick Rate (%)')
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

The kick rate varied quite a bit across size categories. Some sizes had noticeably higher kick rates, which suggested that size is worth keeping as a feature for the model.

Chart 8 - Acquisition Cost by Class¶

In [55]:
plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
df.boxplot(column='VehBCost', by='Class', patch_artist=True,
           boxprops=dict(facecolor='lightgreen'))
plt.title('Acquisition Cost by Class')
plt.suptitle('')
plt.xlabel('Class (0 = Good Buy, 1 = Kick)')
plt.ylabel('VehBCost ($)')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print(f"Median cost - Good Buys: {df.loc[df['Class']==0, 'VehBCost'].median()}")
print(f"Median cost - Kicks: {df.loc[df['Class']==1, 'VehBCost'].median()}")

<Figure size 600x400 with 0 Axes>
Median cost - Good Buys: 6825.0
Median cost - Kicks: 5935.0

Kicked cars tended to be cheaper on average. This makes sense because riskier, more problematic cars are usually the ones nobody wants to pay much for. The lower price could be a signal that something is off with the vehicle.

Chart 9 - Warranty Cost by Class¶

In [56]:
plt.figure(figsize=(6, 4))
df.boxplot(column='WarrantyCost', by='Class', patch_artist=True,
           boxprops=dict(facecolor='lightyellow'))
plt.title('Warranty Cost by Class')
plt.suptitle('')
plt.xlabel('Class (0 = Good Buy, 1 = Kick)')
plt.ylabel('Warranty Cost ($)')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

<Figure size 600x400 with 0 Axes>

Kicked cars tended to have slightly higher warranty costs. This might be because warranty providers recognize riskier vehicles and charge more, or because the types of cars that get kicked happen to be ones with more expensive warranties.

Chart 10 - Correlation Heatmap¶

In [57]:
numeric_cols = df.select_dtypes(include=[np.number]).columns.tolist()
corr = df[numeric_cols].corr()

plt.figure(figsize=(14, 10))
mask = np.triu(np.ones_like(corr, dtype=bool))
sns.heatmap(corr, mask=mask, annot=True, fmt='.2f', cmap='coolwarm',
            center=0, linewidths=0.5, annot_kws={'size': 7})
plt.title('Correlation Heatmap of Numeric Variables')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

The heatmap showed that all the MMR price columns were very highly correlated with each other, most above 0.9. That made sense since they all measure the same car's market value under slightly different conditions. I decided to keep only a couple of them to avoid redundancy. Also, VehicleAge and VehYear were perfectly inversely correlated so I only needed one of them.

Target Variable and Predictors¶

The target variable is Class. It is a binary categorical variable where 0 means good buy and 1 means kick.

Not all variables can be used as predictors. PurchDate is a raw date string that would need conversion. Model, Trim, and SubModel have too many unique values and would create way too many dummy columns. BYRNO is a buyer ID and VNZIP1 is a ZIP code, both with thousands of unique values that would not generalize to new data. VehYear is redundant with VehicleAge. I decided to drop all of these during data preparation.

Data Preparation¶

I needed to clean the data before feeding it into a model. I dropped unnecessary columns, fixed data types, handled missing values and outliers, removed redundant correlated columns, and encoded the categorical variables.

Dropping Unnecessary Columns¶

In [58]:
drop_cols = ['PurchDate', 'Model', 'Trim', 'SubModel',
             'BYRNO', 'VNZIP1', 'VehYear']

df_clean = df.drop(columns=drop_cols, errors='ignore')
print(f"Columns remaining: {df_clean.shape[1]}")
print(list(df_clean.columns))

Columns remaining: 24
['VehicleAge', 'VehOdo', 'MMRAcquisitionAuctionAveragePrice', 'MMRAcquisitionAuctionCleanPrice', 'MMRAcquisitionRetailAveragePrice', 'MMRAcquisitonRetailCleanPrice', 'MMRCurrentAuctionAveragePrice', 'MMRCurrentAuctionCleanPrice', 'MMRCurrentRetailAveragePrice', 'MMRCurrentRetailCleanPrice', 'VehBCost', 'WarrantyCost', 'Auction', 'Make', 'Color', 'Transmission', 'WheelTypeID', 'WheelType', 'Nationality', 'Size', 'TopThreeAmericanName', 'VNST', 'IsOnlineSale', 'Class']

I dropped PurchDate, Model, Trim, SubModel, BYRNO, VNZIP1, and VehYear. The date string was not useful as-is, the next three had too many unique categories, the buyer ID and ZIP code would not generalize, and VehYear was redundant with VehicleAge.

Fixing Variable Types¶

In [59]:
df_clean['WheelTypeID'] = df_clean['WheelTypeID'].astype('object')
df_clean['IsOnlineSale'] = df_clean['IsOnlineSale'].astype('object')

print(df_clean.dtypes)

VehicleAge                             int64
VehOdo                               float64
MMRAcquisitionAuctionAveragePrice    float64
MMRAcquisitionAuctionCleanPrice      float64
MMRAcquisitionRetailAveragePrice     float64
MMRAcquisitonRetailCleanPrice        float64
MMRCurrentAuctionAveragePrice        float64
MMRCurrentAuctionCleanPrice          float64
MMRCurrentRetailAveragePrice         float64
MMRCurrentRetailCleanPrice           float64
VehBCost                             float64
WarrantyCost                         float64
Auction                               object
Make                                  object
Color                                 object
Transmission                          object
WheelTypeID                           object
WheelType                             object
Nationality                           object
Size                                  object
TopThreeAmericanName                  object
VNST                                  object
IsOnlineSale                          object
Class                                  int64
dtype: object

I converted WheelTypeID and IsOnlineSale to object type. WheelTypeID is just a code (1, 2, 3) and IsOnlineSale is 0 or 1 for yes/no. They are not numbers you would do math with, so treating them as categories ensures they get dummy-coded properly later.

Handling Missing Values¶

In [60]:
missing = df_clean.isnull().sum()
missing_pct = (missing / len(df_clean) * 100).round(2)
missing_summary = pd.DataFrame({'Count': missing, '%': missing_pct})
print(missing_summary[missing_summary['Count'] > 0].sort_values('%', ascending=False))

numeric_features = df_clean.select_dtypes(include=[np.number]).columns.tolist()
numeric_features = [c for c in numeric_features if c != 'Class']
categorical_features = df_clean.select_dtypes(include=['object']).columns.tolist()

for col in numeric_features:
    if df_clean[col].isnull().sum() > 0:
        median_val = df_clean[col].median()
        df_clean[col].fillna(median_val, inplace=True)

for col in categorical_features:
    if df_clean[col].isnull().sum() > 0:
        mode_val = df_clean[col].mode()[0]
        df_clean[col].fillna(mode_val, inplace=True)

print(f"\nRemaining missing values: {df_clean.isnull().sum().sum()}")

Empty DataFrame
Columns: [Count, %]
Index: []

Remaining missing values: 0

There were no missing values to fill in this dataset. The code was set up to fill numeric columns with the median and categorical columns with the mode in case there were any, but since everything was already complete it did not change anything.

Handling Outliers¶

In [61]:
outlier_cols = ['VehOdo', 'VehBCost', 'WarrantyCost',
                'MMRAcquisitionAuctionAveragePrice', 'MMRAcquisitionAuctionCleanPrice',
                'MMRAcquisitionRetailAveragePrice', 'MMRAcquisitonRetailCleanPrice',
                'MMRCurrentAuctionAveragePrice', 'MMRCurrentAuctionCleanPrice',
                'MMRCurrentRetailAveragePrice', 'MMRCurrentRetailCleanPrice']

for col in outlier_cols:
    if col not in df_clean.columns:
        continue
    Q1 = df_clean[col].quantile(0.25)
    Q3 = df_clean[col].quantile(0.75)
    IQR = Q3 - Q1
    lower = Q1 - 1.5 * IQR
    upper = Q3 + 1.5 * IQR
    n_outliers = ((df_clean[col] < lower) | (df_clean[col] > upper)).sum()
    if n_outliers > 0:
        df_clean[col] = df_clean[col].clip(lower=lower, upper=upper)
        print(f"{col}: capped {n_outliers} outliers to [{lower:.0f}, {upper:.0f}]")

print("\nDone.")

VehOdo: capped 282 outliers to [31612, 113080]
VehBCost: capped 169 outliers to [1810, 11570]
WarrantyCost: capped 696 outliers to [-302, 2778]
MMRAcquisitionAuctionAveragePrice: capped 450 outliers to [-932, 13048]
MMRAcquisitionAuctionCleanPrice: capped 1543 outliers to [68, 14438]
MMRAcquisitionRetailAveragePrice: capped 261 outliers to [-267, 17295]
MMRAcquisitonRetailCleanPrice: capped 1131 outliers to [584, 19096]
MMRCurrentAuctionAveragePrice: capped 502 outliers to [-886, 12974]
MMRCurrentAuctionCleanPrice: capped 1233 outliers to [102, 14410]
MMRCurrentRetailAveragePrice: capped 233 outliers to [-46, 17582]
MMRCurrentRetailCleanPrice: capped 829 outliers to [1000, 19192]

Done.

Instead of deleting rows with outliers, I used the IQR method to cap extreme values. Anything more than 1.5 times the IQR below Q1 or above Q3 got clipped to the boundary. This keeps all the rows but prevents extreme values from distorting the model.

Removing Correlated Columns¶

In [62]:
mmr_drop = ['MMRAcquisitionAuctionCleanPrice',
            'MMRAcquisitionRetailAveragePrice',
            'MMRAcquisitonRetailCleanPrice',
            'MMRCurrentAuctionAveragePrice',
            'MMRCurrentAuctionCleanPrice',
            'MMRCurrentRetailCleanPrice']

df_clean.drop(columns=mmr_drop, inplace=True, errors='ignore')
print(f"Columns remaining: {df_clean.shape[1]}")
print(list(df_clean.columns))

Columns remaining: 18
['VehicleAge', 'VehOdo', 'MMRAcquisitionAuctionAveragePrice', 'MMRCurrentRetailAveragePrice', 'VehBCost', 'WarrantyCost', 'Auction', 'Make', 'Color', 'Transmission', 'WheelTypeID', 'WheelType', 'Nationality', 'Size', 'TopThreeAmericanName', 'VNST', 'IsOnlineSale', 'Class']

I dropped six of the eight MMR columns since they were all measuring the same thing with correlations above 0.9. I kept MMRAcquisitionAuctionAveragePrice and MMRCurrentRetailAveragePrice to capture key pricing info without the redundancy.

Class Balance¶

In [63]:
balance = df_clean['Class'].value_counts().sort_index()
print("Class distribution:")
print(balance)
print(f"\nKick rate: {balance[1] / balance.sum() * 100:.2f}%")
print(f"Imbalance ratio: {balance[0] / balance[1]:.1f} : 1")

Class distribution:
Class
0    60795
1     6416
Name: count, dtype: int64

Kick rate: 9.55%
Imbalance ratio: 9.5 : 1

The data was not balanced. Good buys outnumbered kicks by about 9.5 to 1. This is common in problems like fraud detection or defect prediction where the bad cases are always the minority. There are techniques to address this like SMOTE (creating synthetic minority samples), random oversampling, random undersampling, or using class weights in the model. I did not apply any of these for this assignment but they could improve performance in the future.

Dummy Coding¶

In [64]:
cat_cols = df_clean.select_dtypes(include=['object']).columns.tolist()
print(f"Categorical columns to encode: {len(cat_cols)}")
for c in cat_cols:
    print(f"  {c}: {df_clean[c].nunique()} unique values")

df_model = pd.get_dummies(df_clean, columns=cat_cols, drop_first=True)
print(f"\nFinal dataset shape: {df_model.shape}")

Categorical columns to encode: 11
  Auction: 3 unique values
  Make: 32 unique values
  Color: 16 unique values
  Transmission: 3 unique values
  WheelTypeID: 3 unique values
  WheelType: 3 unique values
  Nationality: 4 unique values
  Size: 12 unique values
  TopThreeAmericanName: 4 unique values
  VNST: 37 unique values
  IsOnlineSale: 2 unique values

Final dataset shape: (67211, 115)

I used one-hot encoding with drop_first=True to convert categorical columns into binary columns. The drop_first parameter drops one category from each group to avoid the dummy variable trap where one column would be perfectly predictable from the others.

Modeling¶

I built decision trees with different depth limits to find the best balance between underfitting and overfitting. A very shallow tree might be too simple while a very deep tree might memorize the training data.

Train/Test Split¶

In [65]:
X = df_model.drop(columns=['Class'])
y = df_model['Class']

X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(
    X, y, test_size=0.30, random_state=42, stratify=y
)

print(f"Training set: {X_train.shape[0]} samples")
print(f"Test set: {X_test.shape[0]} samples")
print(f"Train kick rate: {y_train.mean()*100:.2f}%")
print(f"Test kick rate: {y_test.mean()*100:.2f}%")

Training set: 47047 samples
Test set: 20164 samples
Train kick rate: 9.55%
Test kick rate: 9.55%

I split the data 70/30 with stratify=y to make sure both sets have the same proportion of kicks.

Model 1 - No Depth Limit¶

In [66]:
dt_full = DecisionTreeClassifier(random_state=42)
dt_full.fit(X_train, y_train)
y_pred_full = dt_full.predict(X_test)

print(f"Tree depth: {dt_full.get_depth()}")
print(f"Number of leaves: {dt_full.get_n_leaves()}")
print()
print(classification_report(y_test, y_pred_full, target_names=['Good Buy', 'Kick']))
print(f"Accuracy: {accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred_full):.4f}")
print(f"Precision: {precision_score(y_test, y_pred_full):.4f}")
print(f"Recall: {recall_score(y_test, y_pred_full):.4f}")
print(f"F1-Score: {f1_score(y_test, y_pred_full):.4f}")

Tree depth: 50
Number of leaves: 5579

              precision    recall  f1-score   support

    Good Buy       0.91      0.90      0.90     18239
        Kick       0.14      0.16      0.15      1925

    accuracy                           0.83     20164
   macro avg       0.52      0.53      0.52     20164
weighted avg       0.84      0.83      0.83     20164

Accuracy: 0.8261
Precision: 0.1372
Recall: 0.1553

In [67]:
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5, 4))
ConfusionMatrixDisplay.from_predictions(y_test, y_pred_full,
    display_labels=['Good Buy', 'Kick'], cmap='Blues', ax=ax)
ax.set_title('Model 1: Fully Grown Tree')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

This was a fully grown tree with no depth restriction. It went very deep and had a lot of leaves, which means it was probably overfitting by memorizing the training data including noise. The confusion matrix showed the breakdown of correct and incorrect predictions.

Model 2 - max_depth = 3¶

In [68]:
dt_d3 = DecisionTreeClassifier(max_depth=3, random_state=42)
dt_d3.fit(X_train, y_train)
y_pred_d3 = dt_d3.predict(X_test)

print(f"Tree depth: {dt_d3.get_depth()}")
print(f"Number of leaves: {dt_d3.get_n_leaves()}")
print()

print(f"Accuracy: {accuracy_score(y_test, y_pred_d3):.4f}")
print(f"Precision: {precision_score(y_test, y_pred_d3):.4f}")
print(f"Recall: {recall_score(y_test, y_pred_d3):.4f}")
print(f"F1-Score: {f1_score(y_test, y_pred_d3):.4f}")

In [27]:
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5, 4))
ConfusionMatrixDisplay.from_predictions(y_test, y_pred_d3,
    display_labels=['Good Buy', 'Kick'], cmap='Greens', ax=ax)
ax.set_title('Model 2: Depth = 3')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

With only 3 levels this tree was very simple. It had high accuracy but it did not predict a single kick. It was just predicting good buy for every car and getting credit for the roughly 90% of cases that really are good buys.

Model 3 - max_depth = 5¶

In [28]:

In [29]:
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5, 4))
ConfusionMatrixDisplay.from_predictions(y_test, y_pred_d5,
    display_labels=['Good Buy', 'Kick'], cmap='Oranges', ax=ax)
ax.set_title('Model 3: Depth = 5')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Depth 5 gave the tree more room to learn patterns but it still barely caught any kicks. The recall was nearly zero, meaning it only flagged a couple of kicks out of nearly 2,000. It was still mostly just predicting good buy for everything.

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5, 4))
ConfusionMatrixDisplay.from_predictions(y_test, y_pred_d7,
    display_labels=['Good Buy', 'Kick'], cmap='Purples', ax=ax)

Depth 7 caught a few more kicks than the shallower trees but still only 9 out of nearly 2,000. The recall was still close to zero. The extra depth did not help much with the core problem of detecting kicks.

Comparing All Models¶

In [34]:
models = {
    'Full Tree (no limit)': (dt_full, y_pred_full),
    'Depth = 3': (dt_d3, y_pred_d3),
    'Depth = 5': (dt_d5, y_pred_d5),
    'Depth = 7': (dt_d7, y_pred_d7),
    'Depth = 10': (dt_d10, y_pred_d10),
}

results = []
for name, (model, preds) in models.items():
    results.append({
        'Model': name,
        'Depth': model.get_depth(),
        'Leaves': model.get_n_leaves(),
        'Accuracy': accuracy_score(y_test, preds),
        'Precision (Kick)': precision_score(y_test, preds),
        'Recall (Kick)': recall_score(y_test, preds),
        'F1 (Kick)': f1_score(y_test, preds),
    })

results_df = pd.DataFrame(results).round(4)
print(results_df.to_string(index=False))

               Model  Depth  Leaves  Accuracy  Precision (Kick)  Recall (Kick)  F1 (Kick)
Full Tree (no limit)     50    5579    0.8261            0.1372         0.1553     0.1457
           Depth = 3      3       8    0.9045            0.0000         0.0000     0.0000
           Depth = 5      5      31    0.9044            0.2857         0.0010     0.0021
           Depth = 7      7     103    0.9021            0.1343         0.0047     0.0090
          Depth = 10     10     385    0.8983            0.1891         0.0197     0.0357

/usr/local/lib/python3.12/dist-packages/sklearn/metrics/_classification.py:1565: UndefinedMetricWarning: Precision is ill-defined and being set to 0.0 due to no predicted samples. Use `zero_division` parameter to control this behavior.
  _warn_prf(average, modifier, f"{metric.capitalize()} is", len(result))

Accuracy looked high for all models but that was partly because of the class imbalance. The more useful metrics were precision, recall, and F1 for kicks. Precision told me how often the model was right when it flagged a kick. Recall told me what fraction of actual kicks the model caught. F1 balanced both. The shallow and mid-range trees had high accuracy but almost zero recall because they barely flagged any kicks at all. The fully grown tree was the only one with meaningful recall and F1 for kicks, even though it was likely overfitting. Overall the results showed that a basic decision tree struggles with this imbalanced dataset. I picked the model with the best F1-score for kicks as the winner, which was the fully grown tree.

Feature Importance¶

In [35]:
best_idx = results_df['F1 (Kick)'].idxmax()
best_name = results_df.loc[best_idx, 'Model']
print(f"Best model: {best_name} (F1 for Kick = {results_df.loc[best_idx, 'F1 (Kick)']:.4f})")

best_models_dict = {
    'Full Tree (no limit)': dt_full,
    'Depth = 3': dt_d3,
    'Depth = 5': dt_d5,
    'Depth = 7': dt_d7,
    'Depth = 10': dt_d10,
}
best_model = best_models_dict[best_name]

importances = pd.Series(best_model.feature_importances_, index=X.columns)
top20 = importances.nlargest(20)

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
top20.sort_values().plot(kind='barh', color='darkcyan', edgecolor='black')
plt.title(f'Top 20 Feature Importances - {best_name}')
plt.xlabel('Importance (Gini)')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Best model: Full Tree (no limit) (F1 for Kick = 0.1457)

The top features were mostly pricing-related (MMR values, acquisition cost) along with vehicle age and odometer reading. This lined up with what I found during the charts where cheaper, older, and higher-mileage cars were riskier. Warranty cost also showed up, which makes sense because pricier warranties often reflect higher perceived risk.

Tree Visualization¶

In [36]:
viz_model = dt_d3 if best_model.get_depth() > 5 else best_model

plt.figure(figsize=(20, 8))
plot_tree(viz_model, max_depth=3, filled=True, rounded=True,
          feature_names=X.columns, class_names=['Good Buy', 'Kick'],
          fontsize=8, proportion=True)
plt.title('Decision Tree (Top 3 Levels)')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

This showed how the tree makes its decisions. Each node splits on a feature at a threshold value and the color shows the majority class at that point. You can trace any path from root to leaf to understand why the model classified a car a certain way. This transparency is one of the main advantages of decision trees over black-box models.

Evaluation¶

The model I selected was the fully grown tree since it had the best F1-score for predicting kicks, though the performance was still low overall with an F1 of about 0.15 and recall of about 0.16. That means it only caught around 15% of actual kicks. Going back to the original business problem, dealers lose money on cars that turn out to have hidden issues. Even with its limitations, this model could serve as a starting point for a risk screening tool at auction time, but it would need significant improvement before being reliable.

In terms of practicality, decision trees are easy to explain to people who are not data scientists. You can show them the tree and walk through the logic. Predictions are also instant once the model is trained, which matters at auctions where decisions happen fast. The feature importance analysis also tells the business which factors matter most, which is useful even beyond the predictions.

The main limitations are the class imbalance, which makes it hard to catch all kicks without also flagging too many good buys, and the fact that a single decision tree can be unstable where small changes in data produce a different tree. For a production system, I would recommend trying ensemble methods like Random Forest, addressing the class imbalance with SMOTE or class weights, and doing a more thorough hyperparameter search.
